text.heading: Nigerians in Diaspora to vote in 2011, says Iwu
text.p: THE Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC), has hinted on the possibility of extending voting rights to Nigerians living abroad in the next general election, in deference to a recent court decision. Before now, it has not been possible for Nigerians in Diaspora to vote in the country's democratic process.

While receiving a delegation from Angola led by that country's Vice Minister for Territorial Administration and Electoral Matters, Edeltrudes Costa in Abuja yesterday, INEC Chairman, Prof. Maurice Iwu, said the consideration followed a recent court pronouncement in Nigeria on the need to give Nigerians outside the country the right to participate in the country's electoral process.

Costa said his team was at the Commission to understudy how Nigeria conducted the 2007 elections that produced President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, noting that Angola was also desirous of having such a successful transition.

The INEC chief told his visitors that certain legal considerations forced the Commission to stop electronic voting because INEC did not want to play into the hands of mischief makers who were looking for an opportunity to foment trouble. But nevertheless, he said, the commission had been mandated to take steps towards enabling Nigerians in the Diaspora to participate in future elections, adding that INEC was already studying how to implement the directive.

According to him, although violence has been associated with elections, excessive use of money in politics as well as a wrong mindset, which makes politicians see elections as a do- or- die affair are issues that still confront the conduct of polls in the country. He said however, that appreciable progress has been made by INEC since the last general elections in 2007.

For instance, according to him, the Commission has moved away from the former practice in which voter's registration was done periodically and has now put in place a system of interactive electronic voter registration. Explaining that with the new arrangement, voter registration is now continuous, Iwu said the Commission would soon publish a list of those who registered in 2008 as required by law.

In a bid to deal with the major challenges confronting the smooth conduct of elections in the country, Iwu said INEC had been spending more money on the education of voters and politicians themselves. He said the problem militating against the electoral process would be solved by the time internal democracy was institutionalised in the parties.

According to Iwu, because the problem would be further reduced to a mere 25 per cent if adequate preparations are made before the main election. INEC has taken steps to ensure that adequate preparations were made ahead of the next elections.
Earlier, Costa had told the INEC chairman that his delegation was in Nigeria to avail itself of useful ideas as it prepares for its local administration elections.
